subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11! In earlier units you learned what a fraction is and how to name simple fractions like one half and one quarter. Now you will take a big step forward. You will learn how to find equivalent fractions, how to compare and order fractions that have different denominators, and for the very first time you will meet percentages. You will also see how fractions, decimals and percentages are really three different ways of describing the very same amount.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify and generate equivalent fractions using diagrams and by multiplying or dividing.
Compare and order fractions, including fractions with different denominators.
Understand that a percentage means ‘out of 100’ and use the % symbol.
Convert between fractions and percentages for common values.
Work with tenths and hundredths and link them to percentages.
See how fractions, decimals and percentages describe the same amount.
Find a percentage of an amount and find the whole when you know a percentage.
Solve word problems involving fractions and percentages in real-life situations.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions
concepts.explanation: Equivalent fractions are fractions that have the same value, even though they may look different. For example, 1/2 and 2/4 are equivalent because they represent exactly the same amount. Think of it like cutting a mango. Whether you cut the mango into 2 equal pieces and take 1 piece, or cut the same mango into 4 equal pieces and take 2 pieces, you end up with the same amount of mango in your hand. We can picture this with a bar split into parts. When a bar is cut in half and one half is shaded, exactly the same…
concepts.example: There is a neat rule for making equivalent fractions without drawing anything. If you multiply (or divide) the numerator and the denominator by the same number, the value of the fraction does not change. Watch what…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
concepts.explanation: To compare two fractions means to decide which is larger, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. When two fractions have the same denominator, comparing is easy: the fraction with the larger numerator is the larger fraction. This makes sense, because the parts are the same size, so more parts means more of the whole. 3/8 < 5/8 because 3 parts is fewer than 5 parts of the same size.
concepts.example: 7/10 > 3/10 because 7 tenths is more than 3 tenths.

concepts.title: What a Percentage Means
concepts.explanation: A percentage is a special way of writing a fraction whose denominator is 100. The word percent comes from words meaning ‘per hundred’, and the symbol we use is %. So a percentage always answers the question: how many out of every hundred? This is the single most important idea in the whole unit, so let us say it clearly: a percentage is a number of parts out of 100. 25% means 25 out of 100, which is the fraction 25/100.

concepts.title: Converting Between Fractions and Percentages
concepts.explanation: Because a percentage is just a fraction out of 100, changing between the two forms is all about the number 100. To change a fraction into a percentage, rewrite it as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100. The new numerator is the percentage. Example: change 1/4 into a percentage. Ask what turns 4 into 100. The answer is 25, because 4 × 25 = 100. Do the same to the top: 1 × 25 = 25. So 1/4 = 25/100 = 25%. In the same way, 1/2 = 50/100 = 50%, and 3/4 = 75/100 = 75%.
concepts.example: Some fraction-to-percentage pairs come up so often that it is worth learning them by heart. Knowing these makes many problems quick:

concepts.title: Tenths, Hundredths, Decimals and Percentages
concepts.explanation: Tenths and hundredths are special fractions that link beautifully to percentages. A tenth is one part out of ten equal parts (1/10). A hundredth is one part out of one hundred equal parts (1/100). Because percentages are all about hundredths, these fractions turn into percentages very easily. To change a tenth into a percentage, multiply the top and bottom by 10 to get a denominator of 100. For example, 1/10 = 10/100 = 10%; 3/10 = 30/100 = 30%; and 7/10 = 70/100 = 70%. To change a hundredth into a percentage is…
concepts.example: There is a third way to write these amounts: as a decimal. Fractions, decimals and percentages are simply three different costumes for the same value. For instance, one quarter can be dressed as the fraction 1/4, the…

concepts.title: Finding a Percentage of an Amount
concepts.explanation: Often we want to find a percentage of a real quantity – for example, 25% of the 20 children in a class. The safest way for now is to turn the percentage into a simple fraction, then find that fraction of the amount. Because you already know the common percentage-fraction pairs, this becomes straightforward. Example: find 50% of 20. Since 50% = 1/2, we need half of 20, which is 10. Example: find 25% of 40. Since 25% = 1/4, we need a quarter of 40, which is 40 ÷ 4 = 10. Example: find 10% of 50. Since 10% = 1/10, we…
concepts.example: You can also work backwards to find the whole when you know a percentage. Suppose 25% of a number is 12. Since 25% is one quarter, the number 12 is one quarter of the whole. So the whole is 12 × 4 = 48. Another example:…

methods.title: How to make an equivalent fraction
steps: Decide the new denominator you want.
Work out what the old denominator was multiplied (or divided) by.
Do exactly the same to the numerator.
Write the new equivalent fraction.

methods.title: How to compare fractions with different denominators
steps: Find a common denominator both numbers divide into.
Change each fraction to that denominator.
Compare the numerators.
Write the correct symbol: <, > or =.

methods.title: How to change a fraction into a percentage
steps: Make an equivalent fraction with denominator 100.
The new numerator is the percentage.

methods.title: Order three fractions
methods.example: Order 1/2, 3/8 and 5/8 from smallest to largest.
steps: Solution: Use 8 as the common denominator.
1/2 = 4/8, and the others are already eighths.
In eighths: 3/8, 4/8, 5/8.
So the order is 3/8, 1/2, 5/8.

methods.title: Fraction to percentage
steps: Solution: What turns 5 into 100?
It is × 20, because 5 × 20 = 100.
Do the same to the top: 3 × 20 = 60.

methods.title: Percentage to fraction
methods.example: Change 35% into a fraction in its simplest form.
steps: Solution: Write it over 100: 35/100.
Both divide by 5: 35 ÷ 5 = 7 and 100 ÷ 5 = 20.
Nothing bigger than 1 divides both 7 and 20, so this is simplest form.

workedExamples.title: Make an equivalent fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look at the denominators. What turns 3 into 12? It is × 4, because 3 × 4 = 12. Do the same to the numerator: 2 × 4 = 8. So 2/3 = 8/12.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find a number that divides both 6 and 8. Both divide by 2. So 6 ÷ 2 = 3 and 8 ÷ 2 = 4, giving 3/4. No number bigger than 1 divides both 3 and 4, so 3/4 is the simplest form.

workedExamples.title: Compare fractions with different denominators
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use a common denominator of 12. Change both: 3/4 = 9/12 and 2/3 = 8/12. Now compare: 9/12 > 8/12, so 3/4 > 2/3.

workedExamples.title: Order three fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Order 1/2, 3/8 and 5/8 from smallest to largest.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use 8 as the common denominator. 1/2 = 4/8, and the others are already eighths. In eighths: 3/8, 4/8, 5/8. So the order is 3/8, 1/2, 5/8.

workedExamples.title: Fraction to percentage
workedExamples.solution: Solution: What turns 5 into 100? It is × 20, because 5 × 20 = 100. Do the same to the top: 3 × 20 = 60. So 3/5 = 60/100 = 60%.

workedExamples.title: Percentage to fraction
workedExamples.prompt: Change 35% into a fraction in its simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write it over 100: 35/100. Both divide by 5: 35 ÷ 5 = 7 and 100 ÷ 5 = 20. So 35% = 7/20. Nothing bigger than 1 divides both 7 and 20, so this is simplest form.

workedExamples.title: Find a percentage of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 75% = 3/4. Find one quarter of 20 first: 20 ÷ 4 = 5. Then three quarters is 3 × 5 = 15. So 75% of 20 is 15.

workedExamples.title: A shopping percentage problem
workedExamples.prompt: A shirt costs 25 shillings. In a sale it is reduced by 20%. What is the sale price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First find 20% of 25. Since 20% = 1/5, one fifth of 25 is 25 ÷ 5 = 5 shillings off. Now subtract: 25 − 5 = 20 shillings. The sale price is 20 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Find the whole from a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: If 30% of a number is 15, what is the number?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First find 10% by dividing 30% (which is 15) by 3: 15 ÷ 3 = 5, so 10% is 5. The whole is 100%, which is ten lots of 10%: 5 × 10 = 50. So the number is 50.

workedExamples.title: A test score as a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: Aisha scored 16 out of 20 in a spelling test. What is her score as a percentage?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Her score is the fraction 16/20. To turn it into a percentage, make an equivalent fraction out of 100. What turns 20 into 100? It is × 5, because 20 × 5 = 100. Do the same to the top: 16 × 5 = 80. So 16/20 = 80/100 = 80%. Aisha scored 80%.

workedExamples.title: A missing percentage
workedExamples.prompt: In a class, 40% of pupils chose football and 35% chose swimming. The rest chose running. What percentage chose running?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The whole class is 100%. Add the two known parts: 40% + 35% = 75%. The rest is 100% − 75% = 25%. So 25% of the class chose running. Always check that all the percentages add up to 100%.

workedExamples.title: Find each amount
workedExamples.prompt: Find each amount: a) 50% of 20 b) 25% of 40 c) 10% of 50 d) 75% of 20.

activities.title: Paper-Folding Equivalent Fractions
activities.materials: a few sheets of paper and a pencil.
steps: Fold one sheet in half and shade one half. Write 1/2 on it.
Fold another identical sheet into quarters and shade two of them. Write 2/4.
Fold a third sheet into eighths and shade four. Write 4/8.
Line up the shaded parts. What do you notice? Write the equivalent fractions you have shown.
You have just proved with your own hands that 1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8.

activities.title: Shade the 100-Square
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and two coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a 10 × 10 grid so it has 100 small squares.
Shade 25 squares in one colour. What percentage is that? What fraction?
Shade another 25 squares in a second colour. Now what percentage is shaded in total?
Ask a family member to name a percentage and you shade the matching number of squares.

activities.title: Percentage Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: food labels, shop receipts, a phone or radio.
steps: Find five percentages in real life (battery level, discount signs, food labels, test scores).
Write each percentage down and say what it means ‘out of 100’.
For each one, write the matching fraction in its simplest form.
Share your five finds with your teacher at the next session.

activities.title: The Market Discount Game
activities.materials: small stones or bottle caps as pretend shillings, and paper price tags.
steps: Set up a pretend market stall with items priced in tens (10, 20, 30, 40 shillings).
One player is the shopkeeper and calls out a discount like ‘20% off’.
The buyer works out the discount and the new price using coins.
Swap roles and play again with different prices and discounts.

activities.title: Fraction, Decimal and Percentage Snap
steps: Make cards showing values in three forms: some fractions (1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/10), some decimals (0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 0.1), some percentages (50%, 25%, 75%, 10%).
Shuffle and deal the cards to two players.
Take turns laying a card down. Shout ‘Snap!’ when two cards show the same value in different forms.
The fastest player keeps the pair. Play until the cards run out.

activities.title: Investigation – Sharing Fairly
activities.materials: 20 small objects (dates, beans or stones) and some family members.
steps: Share 20 dates so that one person gets 50%, one gets 25% and one gets 25%. How many does each get?
Now try 20 dates shared as 10%, 40% and 50%. How many each?
Check that your percentages always add up to 100%.
Make up your own fair-sharing puzzle and give it to a friend to solve.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand equivalent fractions best? How did shading the 100-square help you picture percentages? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Equivalent Fractions Rule
rules.text: Multiply or divide the top and the bottom by the SAME number. 1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8. The value does not change.

rules.title: Comparing Fractions Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: the bigger numerator is the bigger fraction. Different denominators: make a common denominator first, then compare.

rules.title: Percentage Rule
rules.text: A percentage means a number out of 100. The symbol is %. 25% = 25 out of 100 = 25/100 = 1/4.

terms: The top number of a fraction
The bottom number of a fraction
A number out of 100, written with %
Write a fraction with the smallest numbers
commonMistakes: Multiply top AND bottom by the same number

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, a basket of mangoes costs 60 shillings. It is reduced by 20% for Eid. What is the sale price?
realProblems.answer: 1) 20% of 60 = 12 off, so sale price 48 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 20% of 60 = 12 off, so sale price 48 shillings.

selfAssessment: I can identify and generate equivalent fractions using diagrams and by multiplying or dividing.
I can compare and order fractions, including fractions with different denominators.
I can understand that a percentage means ‘out of 100’ and use the % symbol.
I can convert between fractions and percentages for common values.
I can work with tenths and hundredths and link them to percentages.
I can see how fractions, decimals and percentages describe the same amount.
I can find a percentage of an amount and find the whole when you know a percentage.
I can solve word problems involving fractions and percentages in real-life situations.
